title: Establish Authentication and Authorization for Application and NATS Infrastructure
description: Implement a comprehensive authentication and authorization framework for both the application (including all API endpoints) and the NATS messaging infrastructure. This includes establishing user/session authentication for any present or future API routes, protecting diagnostic endpoints such as /api/snapshot, and configuring NATS with client authentication and authorization policies. Ensure that all sensitive endpoints are protected by appropriate access controls and that NATS clients are authenticated and authorized to only the subjects they require.
subtasks: Integrate an authentication framework (e.g., NextAuth.js, Clerk, or custom JWT middleware) into the application, even if initially only applied to diagnostic endpoints like /api/snapshot.
Require an API key or bearer token (from an environment variable) to access /api/snapshot, in addition to NODE_ENV checks.
Implement Next.js middleware to block access to /api/snapshot and similar diagnostic routes in production unless explicitly enabled via environment variable.
Configure NATS server with client authentication (token, user/password, or NKey) and enforce TLS mutual authentication.
Define and apply NATS authorization policies to restrict which services can publish/subscribe to which subjects.
Document the authentication and authorization approach in the project documentation.
Test: Run OWASP ZAP scan against all API endpoints to verify authentication is enforced.
Test: Attempt unauthorized access to /api/snapshot and NATS subjects to confirm access is denied.
acceptance_criteria: All API endpoints (including /api/snapshot) require authentication in production.
NATS server enforces client authentication and authorization policies.
Unauthorized requests to protected endpoints are denied with appropriate error codes.
Security smoke tests confirm /api/snapshot is inaccessible in production unless explicitly enabled.
OWASP ZAP scan reports no unauthenticated access to protected endpoints.
Documentation clearly describes the authentication and authorization mechanisms.

title: Implement Centralized Secrets Management and Secure Credential Handling
description: Deploy and configure a centralized secrets management solution for all sensitive credentials, including NATS credentials, TLS private keys, and any API keys. Ensure that secrets are never stored or documented in plaintext, and that secret rotation and secure retrieval mechanisms are in place. Integrate secrets management with Kubernetes (using Kubernetes Secrets with encryption at rest or an external secrets operator such as HashiCorp Vault or AWS Secrets Manager).
subtasks: Deploy an external secrets operator (e.g., HashiCorp Vault, AWS Secrets Manager) or configure Kubernetes Secrets with encryption at rest.
Store all NATS credentials, TLS private keys, and API keys in the secrets management solution.
Configure automatic secret rotation for NATS credentials and TLS certificates.
Update documentation to describe how to securely retrieve credentials from the secrets manager (never document credentials in plaintext).
Integrate secrets retrieval into application and infrastructure deployment workflows.
Test: Attempt to access secrets via Kubernetes API and verify encryption at rest is enforced.
Test: Rotate a NATS credential and confirm that applications pick up the new secret without downtime.
acceptance_criteria: All sensitive credentials are stored in a centralized secrets manager or encrypted Kubernetes Secrets.
No credentials are present in plaintext in code, configuration files, or documentation.
Secret rotation is automated and tested for NATS and TLS credentials.
Documentation provides secure retrieval instructions.
Tests confirm encryption at rest and successful secret rotation.

title: Harden Kubernetes Infrastructure: Pod Security, Container Hardening, and Image Scanning
description: Apply Kubernetes Pod Security Standards (PSS) at the 'restricted' level across all namespaces, enforce strict securityContext settings on all pods, and integrate container image scanning into the CI/CD pipeline. Ensure all containers (including NATS and application containers) run as non-root, with read-only root filesystems, no privilege escalation, and minimal capabilities. Use only trusted, minimal base images.
subtasks: Enforce Pod Security Standards (PSS) at the 'restricted' level for all Kubernetes namespaces.
Set securityContext on all pods: runAsNonRoot: true, readOnlyRootFilesystem: true, allowPrivilegeEscalation: false, drop ALL capabilities.
Integrate container image scanning (e.g., Trivy, Snyk Container) into the CI/CD pipeline for all images.
Update Dockerfiles to use distroless or minimal base images for all containers.
Document the pod security and image scanning policies.
Test: Deploy a pod with default (insecure) settings and verify it is denied by PSS.
Test: Run image scans and confirm no critical vulnerabilities are present.
acceptance_criteria: All pods are subject to 'restricted' Pod Security Standards.
Pods run as non-root, with read-only root filesystems and no privilege escalation.
Container images are scanned in CI/CD and pass with no critical vulnerabilities.
Only trusted, minimal base images are used.
Documentation describes security policies and enforcement.
Tests confirm enforcement of pod security and image scanning.

title: Enforce Dependency Pinning, Auditing, and Container Image Provenance
description: Implement strict dependency management for both application and infrastructure code. Pin all npm dependencies using --save-exact or .npmrc, commit lockfiles, and use npm ci in CI. Add npm audit to CI pipeline. For container images, reference by SHA256 digest and use only images from trusted registries. Set up automated dependency update tooling (Dependabot or Renovate).
subtasks: Configure npm to use --save-exact for all installs or set save-exact=true in .npmrc.
Commit package-lock.json and enforce npm ci usage in CI workflows.
Add npm audit --audit-level=high to the CI pipeline.
Pin Next.js and all major dependencies to explicit versions (no @latest).
Reference all container images by SHA256 digest, not just tag.
Use only official/trusted images for NATS, OpenTelemetry, Jaeger, etc.
Set up Dependabot or Renovate for automated dependency update PRs.
Test: Run npm audit and verify no high/critical vulnerabilities.
Test: Attempt to build/deploy with an unpinned dependency and confirm CI fails.
acceptance_criteria: All npm dependencies are pinned and lockfiles are committed.
CI uses npm ci and runs npm audit with no high/critical vulnerabilities.
All container images are referenced by digest and sourced from trusted registries.
Automated dependency update tooling is active.
CI fails if unpinned dependencies or vulnerable packages are detected.

title: Configure Ingress Security Headers and Rate Limiting
description: Explicitly configure all critical security headers at the ingress level and define rate limiting parameters for API and static asset routes. Ensure headers such as Content-Security-Policy, Strict-Transport-Security, X-Content-Type-Options, X-Frame-Options, Referrer-Policy, and Permissions-Policy are set. Implement rate limiting to prevent abuse and verify header presence via automated tests.
subtasks: Configure ingress to set Strict-Transport-Security (max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains; preload), X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff, X-Frame-Options: DENY, Referrer-Policy: strict-origin-when-cross-origin, and Permissions-Policy (disable unused APIs).
Implement a Content-Security-Policy with specific directives (script-src, style-src, img-src, connect-src) tailored to the Next.js app.
Define and enforce rate limiting: e.g., 100 requests/minute per IP for API routes, 1000/minute for static assets.
Document the security header and rate limiting configuration.
Test: Use curl -I to verify all headers are present on responses.
Test: Attempt to exceed rate limits and confirm requests are blocked.
acceptance_criteria: All specified security headers are present on ingress responses.
Content-Security-Policy is tailored to the application and blocks unsafe sources.
Rate limiting is enforced as specified for API and static routes.
Automated tests confirm header presence and rate limiting behavior.
Documentation details header and rate limiting configuration.

title: Establish Security Event Logging, Audit Trail, and Anomaly Detection
description: Deploy a security observability stack to capture audit logs, security events, and enable anomaly detection. Aggregate logs from NATS, ingress, and application components. Configure alerting for suspicious activity such as repeated failed auth, rate limit violations, and network policy denials. Ensure logs are shipped to a tamper-evident store with appropriate retention.
subtasks: Deploy a log aggregation stack (e.g., Loki + Grafana, or EFK) alongside Jaeger.
Configure NATS to emit audit logs for connection events and authorization failures.
Configure ingress controller to log all 4xx/5xx responses with client IP, path, and user-agent.
Set up alert rules for unusual traffic spikes, repeated 403/401 responses, and network policy deny events.
Ensure logs are shipped to a tamper-evident store with retention policy.
Document the security logging and alerting setup.
Test: Trigger failed auth and rate limit events and verify they are logged and alerts are generated.
Test: Simulate a network policy denial and confirm it is logged and triggers an alert.
acceptance_criteria: Security event logs are aggregated from all relevant sources.
Audit trails exist for API access, failed auth, and network policy events.
Alerting is in place for suspicious activity.
Logs are stored in a tamper-evident, retained manner.
Tests confirm logging and alerting for key security events.
Documentation describes the observability and alerting setup.
